lines.text: Good morning, Tim. Let’s begin by orienting the keyboard. Welcome to the KeyMaster PRO Course. I’m your tutor — we’ll go step by step, and you’ll always know what to do next.
Good morning, Tim. Welcome back. Let’s continue where you left off.
Good afternoon, Tim. Let’s begin by orienting the keyboard. Welcome to the KeyMaster PRO Course. I’m your tutor — we’ll go step by step, and you’ll always know what to do next.
Good afternoon, Tim. Welcome back. Let’s continue where you left off.
Good evening, Tim. Let’s begin by orienting the keyboard. Welcome to the KeyMaster PRO Course. I’m your tutor — we’ll go step by step, and you’ll always know what to do next.
Good evening, Tim. Welcome back. Let’s continue where you left off.
Welcome to the KeyMaster PRO Course.
I’m your tutor. We’ll go step by step, and you’ll always know what to do next.
Sit comfortably — both feet down, shoulders easy, hands relaxed over the keys. Natural, supported, never forced.
When you’re ready, we’ll begin by orienting the keyboard.
Let’s orient the keyboard first.
Lower notes live to your left, higher notes to your right.
Play any key, and listen to where its sound sits.
Good. That is your first landmark: sound moves across the keyboard, low to high.
That’s clearer. Good. That is your first landmark: sound moves across the keyboard, low to high.
The keyboard is laid out by pitch.
Keys to the left sound lower; keys to the right sound higher.
Play a low note on the left — then a high note on the right.
Good — low on the left, high on the right. You’re hearing the shape of the keyboard.
That’s clearer. Good — low on the left, high on the right. You’re hearing the shape of the keyboard.
Before we play patterns, let’s set up your hands. Good habits now make everything later easier.
Sit tall, a little back from the keys, roughly centred. Let your arms hang easily, elbows about level with the keyboard, shoulders down and relaxed.
Bring a hand toward the keys with the weight carried by your arm, not gripped in the fingers. The wrist stays level — never locked, never dropped.
Now the shape of the hand itself. Let the fingers stay gently rounded, the knuckles slightly up — a soft dome, as if a small ball rested under your palm.
Notice the fingers are different lengths. The middle three — two, three and four — are longer, and reach a little further forward. That matters once we meet the black keys.
Each finger has a number, the same in every method: the thumb is one, then two, three, four, and the little finger is five.
Watch your right hand: one, two, three, four, five.
The left hand uses the same numbers — mirrored. Its thumb is also one, sitting toward the middle of the keyboard.
Watch the left hand: one, two, three, four, five.
Here are both hands together. They use the same numbers, one to five — but they mirror each other.
Both thumbs are finger one, and both sit toward the middle of the keyboard. The little fingers, finger five, reach outward to the ends.
Let’s use a finger on purpose. Your thumb is finger one — strong and close to the keys.
With your right thumb, play any white key near the middle. Let the arm support it — don’t press hard.
That’s finger 1, your thumb — playing with intention. This is how every note begins: a chosen finger, a relaxed hand.
That’s clearer. That’s finger 1, your thumb — playing with intention. This is how every note begins: a chosen finger, a relaxed hand.
Now your longest finger — the middle finger, number three.
With right finger three, play any white key near the middle. Keep the hand curved, the wrist level.
Good — finger 3, curved and supported. Knowing your finger numbers is what lets a teacher (or this Course) guide your hands precisely.
That’s clearer. Good — finger 3, curved and supported. Knowing your finger numbers is what lets a teacher (or this Course) guide your hands precisely.
One last habit before we play patterns: control.
Watch the finger, then press a single key slowly and deliberately — let the arm guide it down, don’t jab. Feel the key under your finger, and listen to the sound it makes.
Good — a slow, supported press. Seeing, feeling and hearing together is how every note is played well. Now your hands are ready.
That’s clearer. Good — a slow, supported press. Seeing, feeling and hearing together is how every note is played well. Now your hands are ready.
A keyboard has white keys and black keys.
The black keys sit in groups of two and three, and that pattern repeats — it helps you find your place.
Here is a group of two.
Now you try — tap either black key.
Good — that’s one of the pair. The groups of two are your first anchor points.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s one of the pair. The groups of two are your first anchor points.
Look again — the two black keys sit close together, with a wider gap before the next group. Tap either one.
Next to the twos are groups of three.
Here is a group of three.
We use these groups as landmarks.
Now you try — tap any of the three.
Good — that’s the group of three. The groups of two and three repeat across the keyboard, so you can always find your place.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s the group of three. The groups of two and three repeat across the keyboard, so you can always find your place.
Look again — the group of three is the wider cluster. Tap any one of the three.
Find a group of two black keys.
The white key just to their left is C.
Because the pattern repeats, C is everywhere.
Now you try — find a C.
Good — that’s C, just to the left of the two black keys.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s C, just to the left of the two black keys.
Let’s look again — first find a group of two black keys, then the white key just to their left is C.
D sits right between the two black keys.
Now you try — find a D.
Yes — that’s D, sitting between the two black keys.
That’s clearer. Yes — that’s D, sitting between the two black keys.
E sits just to the right of the two black keys.
Now you try — find an E.
That’s E, just to the right of the two black keys.
That’s clearer. That’s E, just to the right of the two black keys.
F sits just to the left of the three black keys.
Now you try — find an F.
Good — that’s F, just to the left of the three black keys.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s F, just to the left of the three black keys.
After F comes G — the next white key up.
Now you try — find a G.
Yes — that’s G, the next white key along.
That’s clearer. Yes — that’s G, the next white key along.
After G comes A.
Now you try — find an A.
That’s A. You can now name all of the white keys.
That’s clearer. That’s A. You can now name all of the white keys.
Look near the centre of the keyboard.
Find the two black keys there.
The white key to their left is Middle C.
Here it is.
Now you try.
That’s Middle C — the C nearest the centre of the keyboard.
That’s clearer. That’s Middle C — the C nearest the centre of the keyboard.
Let’s look again — Middle C is near the centre, the white key just left of the two black keys there.
Start from Middle C.
Step one white key to the left.
That note is B — the B below Middle C.
Later, this B becomes the starting note of the B major scale.
Now you try.
Good — that’s B, the white key just below Middle C.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s B, the white key just below Middle C.
Let’s look again — find Middle C first, then step one white key to the left for B.
Moving to the right raises the pitch.
Moving to the left lowers it.
Play two notes that rise — C, then D.
Good — that’s motion upward, C to D. Direction is how a melody moves.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s motion upward, C to D. Direction is how a melody moves.
Moving to the very next key is a step.
Jumping over a key is a skip.
Play C, then skip up to E.
Good — C to E is a skip. Steps and skips are how melodies move.
That’s clearer. Good — C to E is a skip. Steps and skips are how melodies move.
Two useful words now, ones we’ll keep using as scales and keys grow: semitone and tone.
A semitone is the smallest step there is — from one key to the very next key, with nothing between, whether that next key is white or black.
E and F are neighbours with no key between them. Play E, then F — that move is one semitone.
Good — that’s a semitone: the smallest move on the keyboard. Stepping to a black key is a semitone too.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s a semitone: the smallest move on the keyboard. Stepping to a black key is a semitone too.
A tone is simply two semitones — two of those smallest steps joined together.
From C, step up past the black key to D. You’ve moved two semitones — that is one tone.
Good — a tone: two semitones. Scales are built from tones and semitones in a set order, as you’ll see.
That’s clearer. Good — a tone: two semitones. Scales are built from tones and semitones in a set order, as you’ll see.
Before we climb one, a word on why scales matter.
A scale is an ordered set of steps — the basic vocabulary of a key. Melodies, chords and whole pieces are drawn from it, and practising one trains the hand to move evenly and find its shape.
We’ll start in C because its steps are all white keys — easy to see and name. The hand-shape pathway then moves into B major, where the fingers fit the keys especially well.
A scale is steps in order — a ladder of pitch.
Climb the first three.
C, then D, then E.
Good — C, D, E. That’s the opening of a scale: three steps climbing.
That’s clearer. Good — C, D, E. That’s the opening of a scale: three steps climbing.
Let’s climb further — five steps of the C scale.
C, D, E, F, G — each the very next white key.
I’ll play it, then you climb it.
Good — C, D, E, F, G. The first five notes of the C major scale.
That’s clearer. Good — C, D, E, F, G. The first five notes of the C major scale.
A scale comes down as well as up.
From G, step down: G, F, E, D, C.
Now you bring it home.
Good — up and down. You’ve played your first scale, both directions.
That’s clearer. Good — up and down. You’ve played your first scale, both directions.
Those notes moved upward. Now hear a different shape — the opening of B major. Copy the shape: B, then C#, then D#.
That’s the opening of B major — B, C#, D#.
That’s clearer. That’s the opening of B major — B, C#, D#.
B is the white key just left of the two black keys. Climb: B, C#, D#.
You just played the opening of B major.
B major fits the hand beautifully — the black keys give your longer fingers natural resting points.
That’s why KeyMaster PRO grows your playing from a B-major home: it builds confident, ergonomic technique.
You found C and climbed your first steps upward — the beginning of a scale.
A chord is several notes sounded together. Press C, E and G together.
That’s a C major chord — C, E and G together.
That’s clearer. That’s a C major chord — C, E and G together.
You know one chord. Here is a second.
G, B and D, played together, make a G major chord.
Press all three at once.
That’s a G major chord — G, B and D. You now have two chords.
That’s clearer. That’s a G major chord — G, B and D. You now have two chords.
You sounded your first chord — three notes ringing together.
A quick foundation that unlocks written music: the staff.
The staff is the map musicians use to show pitch. Each staff has five lines. A note’s height on the staff tells you how high or low it sounds.
Between the five lines are four spaces.
A note can sit on a line, or in a space. Lines and spaces together give every pitch a place.
At the start of a staff sits a clef — it tells you which pitches the lines and spaces stand for.
The treble clef usually helps us read higher notes — often the right hand at the piano.
The bass clef usually helps us read lower notes — often the left hand at the piano.
Same five lines and four spaces, but standing for lower pitches.
Piano music often uses both clefs together, joined into one system. That is called the grand staff.
Treble on top for the right hand, bass below for the left — to begin with. And right in the middle sits Middle C.
When notes go above or below the five lines, we add small extra lines called ledger lines.
They simply extend the staff. Middle C, just below the treble staff, sits on its own short ledger line — you can see it here.
Now connect it. This note on the staff is Middle C — and Middle C is a key you already know.
Find it on the staff, then find it on the keyboard, and play it.
Good — you connected the written note to the key. That link, staff to keyboard, is the whole of reading.
That’s clearer. Good — you connected the written note to the key. That link, staff to keyboard, is the whole of reading.
Reading music means knowing a note, then playing it.
The note is E. Find it and play it.
Good — you knew E, and you played it. That is where reading music begins.
That’s clearer. Good — you knew E, and you played it. That is where reading music begins.
One more. The note is G.
Find it and play it.
Good — know it, then play it. Cognitive Sight-Reading takes this much further.
That’s clearer. Good — know it, then play it. Cognitive Sight-Reading takes this much further.
Written music lives on lines and spaces — the staff.
Middle C is the shared landmark between your two hands, the note we count outward from.
You already know it on the keys. Play Middle C now, and hold it as your anchor.
Good — that’s your anchor. In Sight-Reading, every note is read from here.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s your anchor. In Sight-Reading, every note is read from here.
Reading grows from recognising landmarks like Middle C, then the patterns around them.
Music moves in time, over a steady pulse — a calm, even heartbeat under the notes.
Music groups beats in different ways. A very common pattern is four beats: one, two, three, four. We’ll start there.
Listen to the count first, then play a note on each beat. Don’t rush.
Good — that’s pulse: an even beat you can rely on. Every piece you play sits on it.
That’s clearer. Good — that’s pulse: an even beat you can rely on. Every piece you play sits on it.
Let’s hold the pulse a little longer — two groups of four.
Listen to the count, then play eight even notes — one on each beat.
Good — a steady pulse you can rely on. Other music counts in twos, threes, sixes and more, but a steady four is a strong place to start.
That’s clearer. Good — a steady pulse you can rely on. Other music counts in twos, threes, sixes and more, but a steady four is a strong place to start.
A quick checkpoint — a chance to use what you already know.
Play Middle C.
That’s Middle C, near the centre — well remembered.
That’s clearer. That’s Middle C, near the centre — well remembered.
Now play the first three steps of the C scale.
Good — C, D, E. The scale is in your hands now.
That’s clearer. Good — C, D, E. The scale is in your hands now.
Last checkpoint — a chord.
Play C, E and G together.
That’s a C major chord. Orientation, names, scales, reading, rhythm and harmony — you’ve begun them all.
That’s clearer. That’s a C major chord. Orientation, names, scales, reading, rhythm and harmony — you’ve begun them all.
That’s Stage 1 complete — the foundations are yours.
You can name the white keys, climb a scale up and down, play two chords, name a note and play it, and hold a steady pulse.
The Course now continues into Stage 2, where we begin shaping these notes into music. The masterclasses are there too, any time, for deeper practice.
Welcome to Stage 2. In Stage 1 you learned the keyboard; now we start to shape those notes into music.
Short phrases, a little harmony, and a steady pulse underneath — one small step at a time.
Here’s something pianists know: B major sits beautifully under the hand.
Your long fingers — two, three and four — fall naturally onto the raised black keys, while the thumb and little finger rest on the white keys. C major actually asks more of a beginner’s flat hand.
C major stays our reference for naming and reading. But B major is the KeyMaster hand-shape pathway — the shape your hand wants to make.
Let’s play the first three notes of B major with the right hand.
Thumb on B — finger one. Then finger two on C sharp, the black key. Then finger three on D sharp, the next black key.
Play them in order: B, C sharp, D sharp — fingers one, two, three.
That’s the B-major hand shape, right hand — thumb on white, long fingers reaching the black keys. It already feels natural.
That’s clearer. That’s the B-major hand shape, right hand — thumb on white, long fingers reaching the black keys. It already feels natural.
The left hand has its own rule in the B family: it begins on the fourth finger.
Finger four on B, finger three on C sharp, finger two on D sharp. The fourth finger is your anchor for the B-major shape.
Play them in order: B, C sharp, D sharp — left-hand fingers four, three, two.
That’s the left-hand B anchor — fourth finger on B. Hand by hand first; both hands together comes later, once each is sure.
That’s clearer. That’s the left-hand B anchor — fourth finger on B. Hand by hand first; both hands together comes later, once each is sure.
A phrase is a few notes that belong together — a small musical idea.
Play C, then D, then E — three steps up.
That’s it — three notes rising, cleanly in order. That ordered shape is the start of a musical line.
That’s clearer. That’s it — three notes rising, cleanly in order. That ordered shape is the start of a musical line.
Now the same idea, coming back down.
Play E, then D, then C.
Good — you kept the notes in order, both directions. Up and back down: a phrase has shape.
That’s clearer. Good — you kept the notes in order, both directions. Up and back down: a phrase has shape.
Reading music is naming notes, then playing them in order — a few at a time to start.
Here is a short pattern: C, then E, then D, then C again. Look at each, then play them in order.
Good — you held the order: C, E, D, C. Reading is this, growing a little longer over time.
That’s clearer. Good — you held the order: C, E, D, C. Reading is this, growing a little longer over time.
Now let’s put notes on the pulse.
Listen to the count of four, then play a note on each beat — four notes, steady.
That’s music on a pulse — notes riding a steady beat. No rush, nothing scored — just the feel of it.
That’s clearer. That’s music on a pulse — notes riding a steady beat. No rush, nothing scored — just the feel of it.
Now a small pattern, kept in time.
Listen to the count of four. Then play four notes, one on each beat — try C, D, E, then C again, kept even.
Good — a pattern sitting on a steady pulse. Notes and time together: that connection is the heart of rhythm.
That’s clearer. Good — a pattern sitting on a steady pulse. Notes and time together: that connection is the heart of rhythm.
A scale isn’t just notes — it’s a shape your hand learns.
Climb the C scale all the way up to the next C: C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C.
That’s the scale shape — a full octave. The Scales Masterclass takes this much further whenever you want it.
That’s clearer. That’s the scale shape — a full octave. The Scales Masterclass takes this much further whenever you want it.
A pattern becomes easy when each note has a finger ready for it.
Right hand: thumb on C — one. Finger two on D. Finger three on E. Then play them in order.
Smooth — each finger ready for its note. That readiness is what fingering gives you.
That’s clearer. Smooth — each finger ready for its note. That readiness is what fingering gives you.
You’ve played C, E and G together — a chord.
Now play the same three notes one at a time: C, then E, then G. That’s an arpeggio.
That’s an arpeggio — a chord spread out in time. Same notes, different feel.
That’s clearer. That’s an arpeggio — a chord spread out in time. Same notes, different feel.
Music is full of small patterns you start to recognise.
Listen to this little shape, then play it back: G, E, C.
You heard the pattern and played it back — that’s musical recognition at work.
That’s clearer. You heard the pattern and played it back — that’s musical recognition at work.
Let’s put it together into a small melody.
Play C, E, G, then the C above — a rising phrase that lands home.
Good — C, E, G, and home. Rising, then settling: that shape is the beginning of a musical phrase.
That’s clearer. Good — C, E, G, and home. Rising, then settling: that shape is the beginning of a musical phrase.
A quick word on practising — because how you practise matters more than how much.
Musicians practise slowly, on purpose. Slow and even builds control; control is what later becomes speed. Rushing only teaches rushing.
Play your melody once more — slower than feels natural, every note even and unhurried.
That’s real practice — slow, even, intentional. This habit will carry you further than any shortcut.
That’s clearer. That’s real practice — slow, even, intentional. This habit will carry you further than any shortcut.
One more musician’s habit: listening.
Play a single note and really listen — hear it begin, then slowly fade. The sound you make is something you shape and notice, not just trigger.
Play any one note, and listen to it all the way until it fades.
That’s listening — the habit behind good tone. You’re starting to connect sound, movement and attention, which is what musicianship is built on.
That’s clearer. That’s listening — the habit behind good tone. You’re starting to connect sound, movement and attention, which is what musicianship is built on.
A calm checkpoint — a chance to bring it together.
Play your melody once more, from memory: C, E, G, C.
Held together from memory — a phrase that’s yours now.
That’s clearer. Held together from memory — a phrase that’s yours now.
That’s Stage 2 — you’re connecting notes, fingers, pattern and pulse, not just finding keys.
Stage 3 brings it together: reading from the staff while your right hand plays. The Course continues — the masterclasses stay here too, any time, for deeper practice.
Welcome to Stage 3. Here we join two things you already have: reading the staff, and playing with your hand.
From now on, when you play, you’ll often see the note on the staff, the key on the keyboard, and the finger to use — all at once. Eye, staff, finger, key, sound.
A landmark to anchor your reading: the note G sits on the second line of the treble staff — the line the treble clef curls around.
That G is the G just above Middle C. Find it and play it with finger two.
Yes — G on the second line. Landmarks like this let you read by sight, without counting every line.
That’s clearer. Yes — G on the second line. Landmarks like this let you read by sight, without counting every line.
Now follow a short rise: C, D, E, climbing from Middle C — each note a step higher on the staff.
Higher on the staff means higher in sound. Play them in order, fingers one, two, three.
There — the notes rose on the staff and your hand rose with them. That match is reading.
That’s clearer. There — the notes rose on the staff and your hand rose with them. That match is reading.
A quick test of direction. Two notes are on the staff — one sits higher than the other.
The higher note on the staff is the higher sound. Play the higher one.
Yes — higher on the staff, higher in sound, further right on the keyboard. Direction connects all three.
That’s clearer. Yes — higher on the staff, higher in sound, further right on the keyboard. Direction connects all three.
Now follow a small pattern with a finger ready for each note: C, D, E, then back to D.
Fingers one, two, three, two. Let your eye lead and your hand follow.
Good — read, fingered, played. The eye-to-hand loop is getting smoother.
That’s clearer. Good — read, fingered, played. The eye-to-hand loop is getting smoother.
A longer line now, still all steps: C, D, E, F, E.
Fingers one, two, three, four, three. Keep the hand quiet — only the fingers move.
That’s it — a five-note line, played in order. Your hand is learning to stay home while the fingers travel.
That’s clearer. That’s it — a five-note line, played in order. Your hand is learning to stay home while the fingers travel.
Now from the top down: G, F, E, D, C — the five-finger position, descending.
Fingers five, four, three, two, one. Even and unhurried.
Good — the five-finger position, top to bottom. This shape underlies the C scale and much more.
That’s clearer. Good — the five-finger position, top to bottom. This shape underlies the C scale and much more.
Let’s add the pulse back. Listen to the count of four, then play four steady notes — C, D, E, F, one on each beat.
Reading, fingering and pulse together now — unhurried.
Yes — reading and playing carried on a steady pulse. That’s how written music actually moves.
That’s clearer. Yes — reading and playing carried on a steady pulse. That’s how written music actually moves.
Bring the B-major shape into your reading. These three notes are B, C sharp, D sharp — the start of B major, right hand.
Thumb on B, finger two on C sharp, finger three on D sharp. The long fingers reach the black keys, just as your hand wants to.
Good — the B-major hand shape, played from the staff. This is the pathway the Scales Masterclass develops in full.
That’s clearer. Good — the B-major hand shape, played from the staff. This is the pathway the Scales Masterclass develops in full.
Let’s shape a longer line: C, E, G, E, C — up through a chord shape and back home.
Fingers one, two, three, two, one. Let it sound like one idea, not five separate notes.
Good — up and home again. Read, fingered, shaped: that is a phrase, and you played it whole.
That’s clearer. Good — up and home again. Read, fingered, shaped: that is a phrase, and you played it whole.
A calm checkpoint — let’s bring this together. Play this short rise once more: C, D, E, F, G.
The whole five-finger position, going up. Fingers one to five.
Good — the five-finger position, played from the staff, top to bottom. Stage 3 is yours.
That’s clearer. Good — the five-finger position, played from the staff, top to bottom. Stage 3 is yours.
That’s Stage 3 — you’re reading from the staff and playing in one motion, right hand leading.
Stage 4 brings in the left hand and the bass clef, and joins them on the grand staff. The Course keeps going.
Welcome to Stage 4. Until now the right hand has led. Now the left hand joins, and with it the bass clef.
We’ll take it hand by hand first — the way good practice always begins — then let them meet on the grand staff.
The bass clef carries the lower notes — usually the left hand at the piano.
Its landmark is F: the note F sits on the fourth line, between the two dots of the clef.
A reminder of the left hand. Same numbers — thumb is one, little finger is five — mirrored, with the thumb toward the middle.
Watch them light: one, two, three, four, five.
A note sits in the bass clef for the left hand. This is C — the C below Middle C.
It sits in the second space of the bass staff. Play it with your left thumb, finger one.
Good — a bass note, played from the staff with the left hand. The left hand reads its own clef.
That’s clearer. Good — a bass note, played from the staff with the left hand. The left hand reads its own clef.
Now a short left-hand rise: C, D, E, low on the keyboard.
The left hand climbs with fingers three, two, one — the thumb arriving on top.
Good — the left hand reads and plays too. Notice it climbs toward the thumb, mirroring the right.
That’s clearer. Good — the left hand reads and plays too. Notice it climbs toward the thumb, mirroring the right.
Here is the grand staff with a note for each hand: a low C for the left, a G for the right.
Play the low note first with the left hand, then the high note with the right. Two clefs, two hands, one keyboard.
There — left below, right above, joined on the grand staff. That division is how piano reading begins.
That’s clearer. There — left below, right above, joined on the grand staff. That division is how piano reading begins.
A first taste of two hands with a job each: the left hand holds a low anchor note, and the right plays a little melody above it.
Play your low left-hand C, then a short right-hand line above. Left grounds, right sings.
Yes — an anchor below and a voice above. You’re hearing how the two hands share a single piece of music.
That’s clearer. Yes — an anchor below and a voice above. You’re hearing how the two hands share a single piece of music.
A reminder that pays off with two hands. Three notes together make a chord; the same three one at a time make an arpeggio.
Play C, E, G one at a time — an arpeggio — with the right hand, fingers one, two, three.
Good — a clean arpeggio. Chords and arpeggios are the same harmony, shaped two ways.
That’s clearer. Good — a clean arpeggio. Chords and arpeggios are the same harmony, shaped two ways.
Toward scales. The right hand’s five-finger position is the front half of the C scale: C, D, E, F, G.
Play it up, fingers one to five — even and connected.
Good — half a scale, evenly fingered. This is the foundation the Scales Masterclass takes all the way.
That’s clearer. Good — half a scale, evenly fingered. This is the foundation the Scales Masterclass takes all the way.
The same shape, left hand, low on the keyboard: C, D, E, F, G.
Ascending, the left hand starts on the little finger: five, four, three, two, one.
Good — the left hand has the shape too, mirrored. Now both hands are ready for scale work.
That’s clearer. Good — the left hand has the shape too, mirrored. Now both hands are ready for scale work.
A word on practising two hands. Always learn each hand on its own first — sure and even — before joining them.
Joining too early just teaches confusion. Hands separately, slowly, then together: that is the whole secret.
Let’s end with a short piece for the right hand — a real little line: C, D, E, F, E, D, C.
Up to F and all the way home. Fingers one, two, three, four, three, two, one. Slowly, evenly, listening.
Good — you played a whole short line from the staff, up and home. That is a real piece of music, played from notation.
That’s clearer. Good — you played a whole short line from the staff, up and home. That is a real piece of music, played from notation.
A calm checkpoint. Play the left-hand five-finger shape once more: low C, D, E, F, G.
Fingers five, four, three, two, one — from memory and from the bass clef.
Good — both hands now know their own clef and the five-finger shape. Stage 4 is yours.
That’s clearer. Good — both hands now know their own clef and the five-finger shape. Stage 4 is yours.
Look how far this has come. You sit and shape your hands, you know the finger numbers, you read both clefs, you play patterns and a short piece, and you feel a steady pulse.

From here the path widens: full scales hand over hand, longer pieces, two hands together. The Scales Masterclass and Cognitive Sight-Reading are ready whenever you want to go deeper — and more Course is on the way.
Here is a small idea. Listen first — then echo it back.
I play two notes: a low one, then a higher one. You answer with the same two.
That is the sound we were looking for — you heard the idea and answered it.
That is the sound we were looking for — you heard the idea and answered it.
Now a short study — a calm line that climbs, then settles home.
Listen first. Then play it evenly, one step at a time: C, D, E, F, G.
A clean line, played evenly. Let it settle under the hand — that is a study doing its work.
A clean line, played evenly. Let it settle under the hand — that is a study doing its work.
Music often asks a question, then answers it.
The line rises like a question, then falls home like its answer. Listen, then play it through.
A question and its answer — rising, then resting home. That is a complete musical idea.
A question and its answer — rising, then resting home. That is a complete musical idea.
In call and response, I play a phrase — and you answer it.
My call rises: C, D, E. Your answer comes back down: E, D, C.
A clear answer — the call rose, and you brought it home. That is a musical conversation.
A clear answer — the call rose, and you brought it home. That is a musical conversation.
A chord is several notes sounding together — a single, fuller colour.
Press C, E and G together, and let the sound ring. Hear how it settles.
A warm chord — three notes becoming one colour. This is the sound that will sit under your melodies later.
A warm chord — three notes becoming one colour. This is the sound that will sit under your melodies later.
One more study — this time a small tune you can almost sing.
Listen, then play it gently: E, D, C, D, E, and up to G.
A small tune, shaped and played whole — that is real music-making, not just notes.
A small tune, shaped and played whole — that is real music-making, not just notes.
Let’s bring something back — no markers this time.
Play a low note, then a higher one. Listen to the difference between them.
You found low and high without help — your ear is leading your hand now.
You found low and high without help — your ear is leading your hand now.
Now find Middle C — this time without the marker.
Let the two black keys near the centre guide you, and play the C just to their left.
Found from the pattern alone — that is the landmark becoming truly yours.
Found from the pattern alone — that is the landmark becoming truly yours.
One short pattern to hold in your ear.
Listen first. Then echo it back from memory — there are three notes.
Heard, held, and played back — that is your musical memory at work.
Heard, held, and played back — that is your musical memory at work.
No need to rush — here is the low-to-high cue again. Take a moment, then play a low note and a higher one.
Here is the Middle C marker again. Take your time, then play it once more.
Let’s hear the pattern once more — then echo it back, in your own time.
